cue: Tell someone to take it slow / easy does it

es.meaning: Slowly, gently; easy does it — softened by the diminutive.
es.example: Despacito, que el piso está mojado.
es.exampleTranslation: Easy now — the floor is wet.

it.meaning: Slowly, gently; little by little; easy does it.
it.example: Piano piano, che il pavimento è bagnato.
it.exampleTranslation: Easy now — the floor is wet.

bridge.note: The same soothing instruction built by two different machines: Italian softens by SAYING IT TWICE (piano piano — reduplication), Spanish by SHRINKING THE WORD (despacio → despacito, the diminutive). Each language reaches for its favorite intensity dial. Both also drift into 'little by little' territory — cross-reference 0234 (a poco a poco / poco a poco), and Mexico even reduplicates the diminutive: poquito a poquito.

interference.es_to_it: Spanish speakers under-use Italian reduplication — it feels childlike to them, but piano piano, pian piano, zitto zitto, sotto sotto are fully adult Italian.
interference.it_to_es: Italians importing reduplication produce 'despacio despacio' — understood, marked; the Spanish softener is the diminutive: despacito.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. The reduplication-vs-diminutive axis generalizes far past this pair — candidate for a themed sequence.
